choices: SUBSTITUTION; PROGRESSION; ANTICIPATION — SUBSTITUTION (a buyer won't pay more than the cost of an equally desirable substitute — the basis of the sales comparison approach); PROGRESSION (a lower-value property's value is pulled UP by surrounding higher-value properties; the inverse is REGRESSION); ANTICIPATION (value reflects the present worth of expected future benefits — the basis of the income approach)
explanation: KEY APPRAISAL PRINCIPLES — frequently tested by definition-matching: (1) SUBSTITUTION — a prudent buyer will pay NO MORE for a property than the cost of acquiring an EQUALLY DESIRABLE SUBSTITUTE; this is the THEORETICAL BASIS of the SALES COMPARISON APPROACH (and underlies the cost approach too); (2) PROGRESSION — the value of a LOWER-value property is ENHANCED (pulled UP) by its proximity to HIGHER-value properties (a modest home in an upscale neighborhood benefits); the INVERSE is REGRESSION — a HIGHER-value property's value is pulled DOWN by surrounding LOWER-value properties (the 'over-improvement' problem); both are subsets of CONFORMITY (value is maximized when a property conforms to its surroundings); (3) ANTICIPATION — value is created by the EXPECTATION of FUTURE BENEFITS; value is the PRESENT WORTH of anticipated future income and/or appreciation; this is the THEORETICAL BASIS of the INCOME APPROACH; (4) OTHER KEY PRINCIPLES: (a) CONTRIBUTION — the value of a component is measured by what it ADDS to the whole (not its cost — a $50k pool may add only $20k); (b) CONFORMITY — value maximized when a property is in harmony with surrounding land uses; (c) SUPPLY AND DEMAND — value rises when demand exceeds supply; (d) HIGHEST AND BEST USE — value reflects the most productive legal/possible/feasible use; (e) CHANGE — value is never static; (f) PLOTTAGE/ASSEMBLAGE — combining parcels can create incremental value; (g) BALANCE — value maximized when land uses are in equilibrium; (h) COMPETITION — excess profits attract competition that may reduce value. The principles of value (especially substitution, anticipation, conformity/progression/regression, and contribution) are among the most heavily tested valuation concepts.

question: A seller's general duty to disclose KNOWN MATERIAL DEFECTS means the seller must disclose:
choices: CONDITIONS KNOWN TO THE SELLER that are NOT readily observable by a reasonable buyer and that would SUBSTANTIALLY AFFECT the value or desirability of the property (or pose a health/safety risk) — e.g., a leaking roof, foundation problems, recurring flooding, faulty systems, prior remediated issues; the modern trend (in most states) has shifted FROM strict 'caveat emptor' TOWARD an affirmative duty to disclose known material defects; 'as is' clauses do NOT excuse this duty, and active concealment is fraud
explanation: SELLER'S DUTY TO DISCLOSE MATERIAL DEFECTS — heavily tested: (1) THE MODERN RULE — most states have shifted AWAY from pure 'CAVEAT EMPTOR' (let the buyer beware) TOWARD an affirmative duty for sellers to disclose KNOWN MATERIAL DEFECTS; many states also mandate a STANDARDIZED DISCLOSURE FORM; (2) WHAT IS A 'MATERIAL DEFECT' — a condition that: (a) is KNOWN TO THE SELLER (the duty extends to known defects — the seller need not investigate, but cannot conceal what they know); (b) is NOT READILY OBSERVABLE by a reasonable buyer on ordinary inspection (LATENT defects carry a stronger disclosure duty than patent/obvious ones); (c) would SUBSTANTIALLY AFFECT the VALUE or DESIRABILITY of the property OR pose a HEALTH/SAFETY risk; (3) EXAMPLES — active roof leaks, foundation/structural problems, recurring flooding/water intrusion, mold (and prior remediated mold), faulty major systems (HVAC, electrical, plumbing, septic), boundary disputes, easements, prior repairs of significant problems, environmental hazards, code violations, unpermitted work; (4) WHAT 'AS IS' DOES AND DOESN'T DO — an 'as is' clause means the SELLER WON'T MAKE REPAIRS, but it does NOT excuse the duty to disclose known material defects, comply with statutory disclosures (e.g., federal lead-paint), or shield against FRAUD/ACTIVE CONCEALMENT; (5) ACTIVE CONCEALMENT (e.g., painting over water stains, hiding damage behind furniture/paneling) is treated as FRAUD regardless of disclosure forms; (6) CONSEQUENCES — rescission, damages, and license discipline for agents who knew and failed to disclose. The duty to disclose known material defects is among the most heavily tested disclosure concepts.

question: The federal lead-based paint disclosure law (Title X) applies to:
choices: RESIDENTIAL 'TARGET HOUSING' BUILT BEFORE 1978 — the law applies to both SALES and LEASES; EXEMPTIONS include housing built in 1978 or later, 0-bedroom units (studios, dorms in some cases), housing for the elderly or persons with disabilities (unless a child under 6 resides/will reside), and certain short-term rentals; the seller/lessor must give the disclosure form, the EPA pamphlet, and (for sales) a 10-day inspection opportunity
explanation: TITLE X (Residential Lead-Based Paint Hazard Reduction Act) — APPLICABILITY (heavily tested): (1) APPLIES TO — RESIDENTIAL 'TARGET HOUSING' built BEFORE 1978 (the year lead-based paint was banned for residential use); applies to BOTH SALES AND RESIDENTIAL LEASES; (2) THE 1978 CUTOFF is the single most important fact — housing built IN 1978 OR LATER is NOT subject to Title X disclosure; (3) EXEMPTIONS (even for pre-1978 housing): (a) HOUSING BUILT 1978 OR LATER; (b) ZERO-BEDROOM units (studio apartments, dormitories in some cases — lofts, efficiencies); (c) HOUSING FOR THE ELDERLY OR PERSONS WITH DISABILITIES (UNLESS a child under 6 resides or is expected to reside there); (d) SHORT-TERM RENTALS of 100 days or fewer with no renewal/extension; (e) housing inspected and CERTIFIED LEAD-FREE by a certified inspector; (f) FORECLOSURE sales (limited circumstances); (4) REQUIRED for covered SALES — (a) the LEAD-BASED PAINT DISCLOSURE FORM (disclose known LBP/hazards, provide available records); (b) the EPA PAMPHLET ('Protect Your Family from Lead in Your Home'); (c) a 10-DAY inspection opportunity (waivable in writing); (d) the contract's Lead Warning Statement and signed acknowledgments; (5) LEASES — disclosure form + pamphlet required, but NO mandatory inspection period (leases just require disclosure); (6) RESPONSIBILITY — both the seller/lessor AND the real estate agent share compliance responsibility; records retained 3 years; (7) NOT REQUIRED — Title X does NOT require testing, abatement, or removal — it is a DISCLOSURE law. The pre-1978 applicability and exemptions are among the most heavily tested federal-disclosure concepts.

question: Whether a 'STIGMATIZED PROPERTY' (one affected by non-physical events such as a death, crime, suicide, or alleged paranormal activity) must be disclosed:
choices: VARIES SIGNIFICANTLY BY STATE — there is NO uniform federal rule; some states require disclosure of certain stigmatizing facts (e.g., a recent violent death); MANY states have 'non-material fact' statutes EXPRESSLY relieving sellers/agents of any duty to disclose deaths or other stigma (often with exceptions for direct questions or for HIV/AIDS, which is also a fair-housing/privacy concern); licensees must NEVER affirmatively misrepresent, but the affirmative disclosure duty is state-specific
explanation: STIGMATIZED PROPERTY disclosure — heavily tested for its STATE VARIABILITY: (1) DEFINITION — a 'stigmatized property' is one whose desirability is affected by NON-PHYSICAL factors — events that don't impact the structure but may affect buyer perception: a murder/violent crime, a suicide, a death (natural or otherwise) on the property, alleged hauntings/paranormal activity, association with a notorious former occupant, or proximity to crime; (2) NO UNIFORM RULE — there is NO federal stigmatized-property disclosure law; the duty (if any) is STATE-SPECIFIC and varies dramatically: (a) SOME STATES require affirmative disclosure of certain facts (e.g., a violent death within a recent period); (b) MANY STATES have 'NON-MATERIAL FACT' STATUTES that EXPRESSLY PROVIDE deaths, felonies, or other stigma are NOT material facts requiring disclosure (relieving sellers/agents of liability for non-disclosure); (c) SOME require disclosure only upon DIRECT QUESTION; (3) HIV/AIDS — federal FAIR HOUSING law (disability) and privacy concerns generally PROHIBIT disclosing that a former occupant had/died of HIV/AIDS — many state statutes specifically address this; (4) THE UNIVERSAL RULE — a licensee may DECLINE to disclose a non-material fact (where state law permits), but must NEVER AFFIRMATIVELY MISREPRESENT or lie if asked directly (an outright false statement is actionable misrepresentation regardless of materiality); best practice is often to refer the buyer to public records (e.g., crime databases, news) or to disclose per the specific state rule; (5) PRACTICAL — licensees MUST know their specific state's stigmatized-property and non-material-fact statutes. The state-by-state variability and the 'never affirmatively misrepresent' rule are the key tested points.

question: The government power that allows ZONING, building codes, and environmental regulations — WITHOUT requiring compensation to affected owners (unless the regulation goes 'too far') — is:
choices: POLICE POWER — the inherent authority of state/local government to regulate private property and conduct to protect public HEALTH, SAFETY, MORALS, and GENERAL WELFARE; zoning, building/housing codes, subdivision regulations, and environmental rules are exercises of police power; NO compensation is owed for valid police-power regulation, UNLESS it is so restrictive that it becomes a 'regulatory taking' (then just compensation is required under the Takings Clause)
explanation: POLICE POWER vs. the other government powers (frequently tested): (1) POLICE POWER — the government's INHERENT authority (held by states, often delegated to local governments) to REGULATE private property use and conduct to protect PUBLIC HEALTH, SAFETY, MORALS, and GENERAL WELFARE; (2) EXERCISES OF POLICE POWER include: ZONING (use, density, dimensional controls), BUILDING and HOUSING CODES, SUBDIVISION regulations, ENVIRONMENTAL regulations, rent control (where lawful), licensing, nuisance abatement; (3) NO COMPENSATION REQUIRED — for VALID police-power regulation, the government does NOT owe affected owners compensation (regulation is not a 'taking'); (4) THE LIMIT — REGULATORY TAKING: if a regulation goes 'TOO FAR' — depriving the owner of ALL economically beneficial use (the LUCAS categorical rule) or, under the PENN CENTRAL balancing test, unfairly burdening the owner considering economic impact, interference with investment-backed expectations, and the character of the government action — it becomes a COMPENSABLE regulatory taking under the Fifth Amendment Takings Clause; (5) DISTINGUISH from the other PETE powers: (a) EMINENT DOMAIN — government TAKES title/property for public use, WITH just compensation (police power regulates use WITHOUT taking title); (b) TAXATION — levies property taxes; (c) ESCHEAT — property reverts to the state when an owner dies without heirs/will; (6) the police power vs. eminent domain distinction (regulation without compensation vs. taking with compensation) and the regulatory-takings limit are among the most heavily tested land-use concepts.

question: A property owner whose lot has a unique HARDSHIP (e.g., an irregular shape) wants to build closer to the side lot line than the zoning setback allows. They should apply for:
choices: A rezoning of the entire neighborhood

A VARIANCE — administrative relief granting permission to DEVIATE from a specific dimensional zoning requirement (setback, height, lot coverage, etc.) due to a HARDSHIP UNIQUE to the property that is not self-created; granted by the zoning board of appeals/adjustment; distinct from a CONDITIONAL USE PERMIT (which authorizes a specifically listed special USE) and from REZONING (which changes the zone classification itself)
explanation: ZONING RELIEF MECHANISMS — distinguishing them is heavily tested: (1) VARIANCE — administrative relief allowing an owner to DEVIATE from a specific DIMENSIONAL (or 'area') zoning requirement — setback, height, lot coverage, lot width, parking — because of a HARDSHIP UNIQUE to the particular property (irregular shape, topography, undersized lot) that is NOT SELF-CREATED, where strict application would deny reasonable use; granted by the ZONING BOARD OF APPEALS/ADJUSTMENT after a hearing; an 'AREA/DIMENSIONAL variance' (most common) differs from a rarer 'USE variance' (permitting a prohibited use — harder to obtain); (2) CONDITIONAL USE PERMIT (also 'special use permit' or 'special exception') — authorizes a use that the zoning code SPECIFICALLY LISTS as permissible in that zone SUBJECT TO conditions (e.g., a church, school, daycare, or hospital in a residential zone) — the use is contemplated by the code, not a deviation from a dimensional standard; (3) REZONING (zoning amendment) — changes the underlying ZONE CLASSIFICATION of the parcel(s) (e.g., from R-1 to C-1); a LEGISLATIVE act requiring planning commission review, public hearings, and governing-body approval; 'spot zoning' (rezoning a small parcel inconsistently with the area/plan) is disfavored; (4) NONCONFORMING USE — a 'grandfathered' use/structure that LEGALLY existed before a zoning change now prohibiting it; allowed to continue but typically cannot be expanded/rebuilt/resumed after abandonment; (5) KEY EXAM DISTINCTION — VARIANCE = deviation from a DIMENSIONAL standard due to property-specific HARDSHIP; CONDITIONAL USE = a code-listed special USE with conditions; REZONING = changing the zone itself; NONCONFORMING = pre-existing grandfathered use. Distinguishing these four is one of the most heavily tested land-use concepts.

question: Match the leasehold (non-freehold) estate to its definition: a lease for a definite, fixed term that ends automatically with no notice = ___; a tenancy that automatically renews period-to-period until proper notice = ___; a tenant who remains after the lease ends without the landlord's consent = ___.
choices: Tenancy at will; Estate for years; Periodic tenancy

ESTATE FOR YEARS; PERIODIC TENANCY; TENANCY AT SUFFERANCE — an ESTATE FOR YEARS (tenancy for years) has a definite start and end date and terminates automatically with NO notice required; a PERIODIC TENANCY (e.g., month-to-month) automatically renews each period until either party gives proper statutory notice; a TENANCY AT SUFFERANCE arises when a holdover tenant stays after lease expiration WITHOUT the landlord's consent (distinct from a TENANCY AT WILL, which continues with both parties' consent until either terminates)
Periodic tenancy; Tenancy at sufferance; Estate for years
explanation: LEASEHOLD (NON-FREEHOLD) ESTATES — the four types, frequently tested by definition-matching: (1) ESTATE FOR YEARS (also 'tenancy for years' or 'term tenancy') — a lease for a DEFINITE, FIXED period with a specified BEGINNING and ENDING date (e.g., 'January 1 to December 31' or a 2-year lease); it TERMINATES AUTOMATICALLY at the end of the stated term — NO NOTICE is required by either party (the date itself ends it); does not need to be a year — any fixed term qualifies; (2) PERIODIC TENANCY (also 'estate from period to period') — continues for successive PERIODS (month-to-month, week-to-week, year-to-year) and AUTOMATICALLY RENEWS each period UNTIL either party gives PROPER NOTICE of termination (notice length is set by statute/lease — commonly 30 days for month-to-month); often arises when a tenant holds over and the landlord accepts rent; (3) TENANCY AT WILL — continues for an INDEFINITE period at the WILL of BOTH parties; either party may terminate at any time (statutes typically still require some notice — often 30 days); arises by express agreement or implication (e.g., occupying with permission but no defined term); (4) TENANCY AT SUFFERANCE — the LOWEST estate; arises when a tenant who lawfully entered REMAINS in possession AFTER the lease expires WITHOUT the landlord's consent (a 'holdover'); the landlord may either evict (treat as a trespasser) OR elect to treat the tenant as a holdover (often charging holdover/double rent); accepting rent can convert it to a periodic tenancy; (5) KEY DISTINCTIONS — ESTATE FOR YEARS = fixed term, auto-ends, NO notice; PERIODIC = auto-renews until notice; AT WILL = indefinite, both parties' consent, terminable at will; AT SUFFERANCE = holdover WITHOUT consent. The four leasehold estates and their notice/termination rules are among the most heavily tested leasing concepts.
